stories.headline: HORMUZ CEASEFIRE FRACTURES: SATURDAY IS DEADLIEST DAY IN STRAIT SINCE CRISIS BEGAN
stories.body: Iran fired on at least three commercial tankers Saturday and re-closed the Strait of Hormuz hours after briefly declaring it open Friday — a move that had sent Brent tumbling over 10% intraday toward $95. Trump called the attacks a 'total violation' of the truce and renewed threats to strike Iranian infrastructure. Brent is set to re-test $105–$106 at Monday's open as the geopolitical risk premium re-prices.

stories.headline: BRENT WHIPSAWS 10%+ IN 24 HOURS AS HORMUZ NARRATIVE FLIPS TWICE
stories.body: Friday's ceasefire-open announcement sent Brent from ~$106 toward $95 intraday before the re-closure reversed the move entirely. WTI closed at $96.07 (+2.6% on Friday) as the U.S. market held more skepticism. Saturday's tanker attacks confirm the risk premium is structural rather than transitory — the IEA labels this the largest oil-market disruption in history, with roughly 20% of global crude flow physically offline.

stories.headline: IMF CUTS GLOBAL GROWTH TO 3.1% IN 'GLOBAL ECONOMY IN THE SHADOW OF WAR' OUTLOOK
stories.body: The IMF's April 2026 World Economic Outlook downgraded 2026 global growth to 3.1% (from ~3.4%) and lifted headline inflation to 4.4%. The adverse scenario — sustained Hormuz disruption — puts growth at 2.5% with inflation above 5.4%. The 10-year Treasury closed Friday at 4.38% (+7 bps) as breakevens widen and the term premium expands, consistent with the IMF's stagflation framing.

stories.headline: FED STILL PENCILED IN ONE CUT AS POWELL FACES SUPPLY-SHOCK DILEMMA
stories.body: The FOMC held at 3.50–3.75% in March and the dot plot still projected one 2026 cut, but fed funds futures now imply the first cut pushed to September or later. Powell's challenge: a classic supply shock lifts inflation and hurts growth simultaneously — the textbook 'do nothing and look through it' answer is politically harder when headline CPI is visibly spiking at the pump.

stories.headline: GOLD COOLS FROM RECORD AFTER $4,738 PEAK; DXY BOUNCES ON SAFE-HAVEN BID
stories.body: Spot gold slipped ~1% to $4,689 after printing an all-time high of $4,738 earlier in the week, as the dollar index rebounded +0.3% to 98.82 on safe-haven demand and higher real yields. The move looks like tactical profit-taking rather than a regime change — gold's year-to-date move remains the dominant macro trade of 2026, up dramatically on stagflation fears.

stories.headline: NBA DRAFT DECLARATION WINDOW CLOSES; PORTAL LOCKED — OFF-SEASON ROSTER RESHUFFLES COMPLETE
stories.body: The April 24 underclassman declaration deadline passed at 11:59 PM ET, officially closing the spring roster movement period. The transfer portal window shut April 21. Top portal final commitments: 7-foot-2 center Moustapha Thiam to Michigan (reigning national champions), guard John Blackwell from Wisconsin to Duke, and 19.9 PPG scorer Terrence Brown from Utah to North Carolina. Programs now have largely locked rosters for 2026-27.

stories.headline: MAGIC STUN #1-SEED PISTONS 112-101 IN GAME 3 TO TAKE 2-1 SERIES LEAD
stories.body: Orlando's eighth seed stunned Detroit 112-101 in a physical Game 3 at Kia Center, taking a 2-1 series lead after Cade Cunningham was limited and the Magic's defense stifled Detroit's halfcourt attack. Celtics beat Sixers 108-100 (Tatum 25, Brown 25 vs. Maxey's 31) to go up 2-1. LeBron's Lakers added a road stunner in Houston. Hawks-Knicks Game 4 and Wolves-Nuggets Game 4 tip tonight.

stories.headline: NFL DRAFT DAY 3: McCOY FALLS TO RAIDERS; SEAHAWKS CLOSE FOUR-PLAYER HAUL
stories.body: Tennessee CB Jermod McCoy, a consensus late-first-round talent, fell to the Las Vegas Raiders at the top of Round 4 on knee injury concerns — potentially the steal of the entire class. Seattle finished Day 3 with sixth-round additions (picks #188, #216), capping a draft that addressed RB (Jadarian Price, #32), S (Bud Clark, #64), and CB (Julian Neal, #99). Carson Beck's Round 3 landing with the Cardinals drew the sharpest analyst criticism of the weekend.

stories.headline: SPRING WRAPS AS PROGRAMS FINALIZE DEPTH UNDER SINGLE-WINDOW PORTAL REGIME
stories.body: Spring practice is concluding nationwide under the first off-season without a spring transfer portal window — the NCAA's new single-window rule forces roster construction into January, ending years of spring churn. UW moved a session to the Seahawks' VMAC facility under HC Jedd Fisch, who is also now serving as OC. The NCAA is expected to vote soon on tougher penalties for schools using post-spring 'withdrawal' workarounds to circumvent the new rules.

sports.detail: Lost Game 3 to Magic 101-112 in Orlando. Cunningham limited; Magic defense dominant. Trail series 1-2. Must-win Game 4 Monday in Orlando.
Beat Cardinals 3-2 Friday; Naylor solo HR (418 ft, 6th inning) off Pallante was the difference. Kirby strong start. Season 12-15. Game 3 vs. Cardinals today, series finale Sunday.
R6 picks #188 and #216 on the clock today. Draft haul: Price (RB, #32), Clark (S, #64), Neal (CB, #99); added OL depth in 6th round per Seattle Times report.
Practice session moved to Seahawks VMAC facility. HC Jedd Fisch also serving as OC after Dougherty departure. Safety recruit Pole Moala reclassified to fast-track eligibility.
3-star SF Lattimore Ford committed as first 2026 recruiting class addition. Portal adds Friedrichsen (Davidson G) and Venters (Gonzaga G, 36.7% 3PT) locked in. Roster rebuild ongoing.

watchlist: Core PCE Thursday 4/30 8:30 AM ET — watch for oil pass-through into core services ex-housing
May 5-6 FOMC — no cut expected; statement language on supply-shock 'transitory' framing is the key signal
Pistons vs. Magic Game 4 Monday in Orlando — series 1-2 Detroit, near elimination pressure
NFL Draft Day 3 wrapping today — Seahawks R6 picks #188 and #216 on the clock
Mariners vs. Cardinals series finale Sunday — bullpen depth tested after back-to-back one-run games

deepDive.title: The Oil Risk Premium: How Fear Gets Priced Into Crude
content: Friday's Hormuz-open announcement sent Brent tumbling over 10% intraday — one of the largest single-session crude moves in years. By Saturday, that gain was erased as Iran fired on three tankers and re-closed the strait. The 24-hour round-trip illustrates something specific: crude oil markets embed two distinct prices simultaneously — the physical supply-and-demand price, and a geopolitical risk premium that can move independently and violently. Understanding how that premium is sized, traded, and eventually realized (or not) is essential to reading today's energy tape, because the Brent number on screen is not a single consensus about reality — it is the output of competing probability models about escalation.

The risk premium is the spread between what oil would cost in a stable supply environment and its actual quoted price. Traders isolate it by monitoring the OVX (CBOE Crude Oil Volatility Index, oil's VIX equivalent) and by reading the futures curve shape. When supply disruption is anticipated, the curve enters backwardation — front-month contracts price above later months, signaling urgency for physical barrels. Brent's M1-M3 spread (front month minus three months out) exceeded $6/bbl at peak Hormuz stress. Options also price the premium: 25-delta risk reversals on Brent skew sharply toward calls during crises, meaning the market pays far more to hedge upside price risk than downside — the reverse of equities, where puts typically carry the structural premium.

The textbook error is treating the risk premium as binary — but it is a probability-weighted expected value across future supply scenarios. During the 1990 Gulf War, Brent premium peaked near $15–$20/bbl then evaporated in a single session when the air campaign proved faster than expected. Iran's 2013 Geneva interim deal knocked ~$5/bbl off Brent within 48 hours — no physical supply change, only a revised probability distribution. Today differs materially: the IEA has confirmed ~10 mb/d of actual physical disruption, meaning a portion of the ~$30/bbl premium above pre-war Brent reflects real supply loss rather than pure sentiment. The hard work for traders is disaggregating how much of current WTI prices is permanent structural tightness versus headline-driven anxiety.

Friday's compression trade — sell oil on ceasefire news — and Saturday's violent reversal will be studied in risk-management courses for years. The key forward signal is the Brent M1-M3 spread: if it narrows materially below $6/bbl, the forward curve is pricing medium-term resolution regardless of daily headlines, which is structurally bearish for crude. Watch core PCE Thursday (4/30): if core services ex-housing is rising, the Fed will read oil pass-through and hold the rate path higher even as growth softens. That is precisely the scenario the IMF's 3.1% growth / 4.4% inflation base case implies — oil elevated, equities pressured, bonds selling off simultaneously. It is the most difficult allocation environment in modern portfolio management, and right now the futures curve says it is not going away soon.
